title: DNA Replication
description: Discover how cells copy their genetic code before dividing.

steps.speech: Before a cell can divide, it must make an exact copy of its DNA. This process is called DNA replication, and it involves a team of incredible microscopic machines.
It starts with the original, double-stranded parental DNA. The two strands are held together by complementary base pairs.
To copy the DNA, we first have to unzip it! An enzyme called Helicase acts like a wedge, breaking the bonds between base pairs and creating a replication fork.
Once unzipped, DNA Polymerase swoops in. This enzyme reads the exposed bases and attaches matching nucleotides to build the new strands.
Because DNA is directional, one strand is built continuously, following the helicase. This is the leading strand.
The other strand is built backwards in short chunks called Okazaki fragments. This is the lagging strand. Eventually, these fragments are glued together.
By the end of the process, one parental helix becomes two identical daughter helices, ready for cell division!
